problem: Each block on the grid shown in the Figure is 1 unit by 1 unit.  Suppose we wish to walk from $A$ to $B$ via a 7 unit path, but we have to stay on the grid -- no cutting across blocks.  How many different paths can we take?[asy]size(3cm,3cm);int w=5;int h=4;int i;for (i=0; i<h; ++i){draw((0,i) -- (w-1,i));}for (i=0; i<w; ++i){draw((i, 0)--(i,h-1));}label("B", (w-1,h-1), NE);label("A", (0,0), SW);[/asy]

classified_questions.question: Can you explain the problem statement in your own words?
classified_questions.label: f

classified_questions.question: How many total steps do we need to take to go from point A to point B?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: How many steps do we need to go to the right? How many steps do we need to go up?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: Why must our path consist of 4 steps to the right and 3 steps up? Can we have a different configuration?
classified_questions.label: r

classified_questions.question: How many different ways can we arrange the steps to the right and the steps up?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: Can you explain why we need to choose 3 out of the 7 steps to be "up"?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: How many ways can we choose 3 steps to be "up" out of 7 steps in total?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: Can you calculate the value of $\binom{7}{3}$? What does this value represent?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: How can we simplify the expression $\frac{7 \times 6 \times 5}{3 \times 2 \times 1}$?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: What is the final result? How many different paths can we take from point A to point B?
classified_questions.label: b
